book_claims.mechanism: Citrus fruits are among the best dietary vitamin C sources; vitamin C supports collagen synthesis, immune function, and iron absorption; folate is required for DNA synthesis and methylation.
book_claims.text: Orange juice provides folate (35 mcg per ¾ cup, 9% DV), vitamin C (93 mg per ¾ cup, 155% DV), and — when fortified — calcium (261 mg per 6 oz, 26% DV). A medium fresh orange provides 70 mg vitamin C (117% DV) and 29 mcg folate (7% DV).

supplementary_research.source: Cassidy et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (2012) — PMID 22218162
supplementary_research.finding: High dietary intake of flavanones — the dominant polyphenol class in oranges — was associated with a 19% reduction in ischaemic stroke risk in women over 14 years of follow-up, independent of other dietary factors.

A meta-analysis of prospective cohort studies found each additional serving of fruit per day associated with a 3% lower all-cause mortality; citrus fruits including oranges showed particularly strong associations with reduced cardiovascular disease mortality.

supplementary_research.source: Scalbert et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (2005) — PMID 15640510
supplementary_research.finding: Hesperidin and narirutin, the principal flavanones in oranges, inhibit LDL oxidation, improve endothelial function, and reduce plasma triglycerides in clinical trials — mechanisms directly relevant to cardiovascular longevity.

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Water-soluble; highly bioavailable from whole fruit; degrades with heat, light, and storage; best consumed fresh.
Primarily in peel and white pith; hydrolysed by gut bacteria to hesperetin; anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective.
Naturally occurring 5-methyltetrahydrofolate; good bioavailability from fresh orange; reduced by heat processing.
Calcium absorption from fortified orange juice is comparable to dairy milk (~36%); useful for those who avoid dairy.

synergies.note: Vitamin C from orange juice or fresh orange consumed with a legume meal increases non-heme iron absorption by 2–6-fold by reducing ferric iron to the absorbable ferrous form.
Orange's flavanones and olive oil's oleocanthal both inhibit NF-κB inflammatory pathways; the Mediterranean citrus-olive pairing amplifies cardiovascular protection.
Classic Sicilian pairing; the anise notes of fennel balance the sweet acidity of orange; both contribute anti-inflammatory polyphenols.
